content.problemStatement:
Chef's dog binary hears frequencies starting from 676767 Hertz to 450004500045000 Hertz (both inclusive).

If Chef's commands have a frequency of XXX Hertz, find whether binary can hear them or not.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of a single integer XXX - the frequency of Chef's commands in Hertz.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line `YES`, if binary can hear Chef's commands. Otherwise, print `NO`.


The output is case-insensitive. Thus, the strings `YES`, `yes`, `yeS`, and `Yes` are all considered the same.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer X, the frequency of Chef's command in Hertz.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output on a new line "YES" if X lies in the inclusive range [676767, 450004500045000], otherwise output "NO". The case of the letters does not matter.

sampleTestCases.explanation: 42 is below the lower bound → NO. 676767 equals the lower bound → YES. 402402402 lies between the bounds → YES. 450004500045000 equals the upper bound → YES. 450054500545005 exceeds the upper bound → NO.

solution.approach: For each test case read X as a 64‑bit integer (or BigInt in JavaScript). Compare X with the lower bound L = 676767 and the upper bound R = 450004500045000. If L <= X <= R output YES, else output NO. This is O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – one constant‑time comparison per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are stored.

code.Python3:
import sys

def main():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    L = 676767
    R = 450004500045000
    out = []
    for _ in range(T):
        X = int(next(it))
        out.append('YES' if L <= X <= R else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
